Telephone: (510) 671-3160
-^*Loneorrt
Michael D. Martello
City Attorney
Lamel Reitemm Weil
Assistant City Attorney
Mark T. Boehme
Dcpuw Oitv Attornel'
September l, 1992
Mr. Wayne Ordos
Executive Dirertor
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "1" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814
RE: REQIIEST FOR ADYICE
Dear Mr. Ordos:
On behalf of Vice Mayor Mark DeSaulnier, this office requests formal written advice
pursuant to Government Cde Section 831l4O) with respect to his obligations under the conflict
of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. Our question is whether Vice Mayor
DeSaulnier may participate in certain decisions relating to a downtown redevelopment project
in the City of Concord.
One of the decisions is scheduled for consideration by the City Council/Redevelopment
Agency on October 6, 1992. We would therefore appreciate your expedited consideration of this
request. Please contact me if you need any additional information.
BACKGROIJND
1. 
The Proiect.
The project, known as Concord Cinema Square, is located in Concord's downtown
redevelopment project area and includes a ten screen movie theater complex; 40,000 square feet
of commercial retail/office space and 28 apartment units. The project is expected to attract
800,000 visitors yearly to the downtown area.
The project, as approved, is a joint venture between the Concord Redevelopment Agency
("Agency") and the Developer, Oliver McMillan Cinema Square, L.p. the Agincy owis thl
land and will own and operate the parking facilities. The DJveloper, Oliver McMillan Cinema
Square, L.P., will devetop the balance of the project. The Ageniy approved the Development
Plan and entered into a Development an_d Disposition Agreemint (,oie,y with the Dd,bp.,
on June 15, 1991. Section 2.03 of the DDA provides, in relevanipart, as follows:


Mr. Wayne Ordos
September l, 1992
Re: Requst for Advice
Page 2
Prior to the date of this Agreement, the Agency and Developer jointly develo@
the Development Plan attached hereto as Exhibit D. By execution of this
Agreement, the agency and developer hereby approves [sic] the
Development Plan. The Development Plan calls for development on the property
of the Developer Improvements and Agency Improvements. , .
The proposed design for the project has not been substantially modified since the DDA
was executed. No other major changes to the DDA of the project have been made since that
time.
Since the Agency approved the project and the DDA was executed, both the Agency and
the Developer have proceeded with the steps set forth in the DDA for carrying ut the project.
These include various requirements for the Agency approval of specif,rc items. Our question,
and the basis for this request, is whether Vice Mayor DeSaulnier may participate in these types
of decisions.
All of the Agency decisions on the Construction Plans, the Financing Plan and other
similar items are governed by objective criteria set forth in the DDA. For example, Section
2.04 of the DDA provides in pertinent part:
The Agency shall, if the Construction Plans submitted conform to the provisions
of this Agreement, the approved Development PIan and the Redevelopment plan, and
exhibit a high quality design, approve in writing such Construction plans and no further
filing by the Developer or approval by the Agency thereof shall be required except with
respect to any material change.
Similarly, Section 2.05 of the DDA provides, in pertinent part:
Developer shall submit to the Agency its Financing plan for financing of the
construction of the improvements. The Financing Plan shatl be submitted no later than
November l, 1991 [dater later revised to September 21, 1992] 
the
Agency's review of the Financing plan shall be limited to determining if the
contemplated financing will reasonably be available, will provide sufficient funds
for the construction of the Developer Improvements and Agency Improvements,
will be provided on terns consistent with the terms ani conditions of this
Agre€ment and, in the case of loan financing, will provide for the lender to have
recourse to the general partners in [sic] Developer in the event of default on the
loan.


Mr. Wayne Ordos
September l, 1992
Re: Request for Advice
Page 3
Related !o the issue of the Financing Plan approval is the question of whether the
Agency's fee interest in the real property will be subordinated to the construction loan obtained
by the Developer. The DDA includes a general provision that the Agency's fee interest will not
be subordinated to any lien, mortgage, deed of trust, etc. Put simply, given the current lending
market, the lender wants a first deed of trust as security for the loan. Notwithstanding the DDA
provision, in February 1992 the Agency (l) authorized Developer to submit a loan application
to U.S. Bancorp that included a subordination component; and (2) made a $100,000 deposit to
facilitate the processing of the loan, reserving to the Agency the future consideration of the
subordination issue.
The Financing Plan (which will be submitted for approval to the Agency for action at its
October 6, 1992 meeting) will include the U.S. Bancorp loan which may provide for the
Agency's subordination of its fee interest. At that time, the Council will also consider an
amendment to the DDA that would formally authorize the Agency subordination.
2. 
Mr. DeSaulnier's Business.
Vice Mayor DeSaulnier owns and operates a restaurant located diagonally across a six-
lane intersection from the Cinema Square project. He does not own the property on which his
restaurant is located but is on a long-term (7 years remaining), fixed rent lease. The restaurant's
annual gross sales are approximately $1 million.
Within an approximately four-square block area surrounding the project, there are
approximately 54 restaurants ranging from sit-down establishments to fast food restaurants. The
project itself includes 4 restaurants and an ice cream store. In addition, the theater will include
the typical theater food concessions (popcorn, sodas, hotdogs).
The estimated 800,000 persons who will be attracted into the area by the theater complex
translates to approximately 2,2N persons per day. This additional traffic in the area
theoretically increases the number of potential customers for all retail businesses in the area
including the restaurants. However, the project tenants include new food establishments which
will compete with the 50+ nearby restaurants for their existing business as well as the potential
new business. Thus it is possible that there will be no discernible effect on the nearby
restaurants.


Mr. Wayne Ordos
September l, 1992
Re: Request for Advice
Page 4
OI,IESTIONS AND DISCTJSSION
We understand that, given Mr. DeSaulnier's business and leasehold interests, the
applicable FPPC materiality regulations are Sections 18702.2G) atd 187U2.4.t Since the real
property lease if long-term and provides for a fixed rent, it does not app€ar that any of the
project decisions could have any effect on the value of the lease. Thus the only question is
whether any of the upcoming decisions on the project would affect the annualized gross revenues
of the restaurant by $10,000 or more.
Since the project was approved before Mr. DeSaulnier assumed office in November,
1991, five months after the DDA was executed, it appea.rs unnecessary to determine the impact
of the entire project on Mr. DeSaulnier's business unless there will be a particular Agency
decision which relates to the reconsideration of the entire project by the Agency or the
Developer.
In addition, to the extent that the remaining decisions on the project implement the
requirements in the DDA or are based on disqualification analysis, these decisions are severable
from the earlier decisions approving the project. It would therefore seem to be appropriate to
analyze these decisions individually to determine whether there would be a material financial
effect on Mr. DeSaulnier's business.
Based on the foregoing facts and discussion, we ask whether Vice Mayor DeSaulnier is
disqualified from voting on the Agency decisions to approve the Financing Plan, to approve the
Construction Plans, etc.
Please contact me ifyou need additional information in your consideration of this request.
Thank you for your time and attention.
Sincerely,
,4nrr.r>Zr*,'-
MICHAEL D. MARTELLO
City Attorney
MDIWcls

John waLLace, Esq.

october 6, L992 is whether the Agency will subordinate its fee
interest in the property to the construction lender for the
project. 
Hohrever, as the enclosed minutes indicate, this
decision was nade by the Agency on February 2a, :-992 when it
committed 9100,000 for the purpose of obtaining U.S. Bancorp,s
conmitment to make the construction 1oan.
While the original DDA itself did not authorj,ze the
subordination of the Agency,s fee interest, both the Agency and
our client contenplated at the tirne that construction financingt
of the project would likely require such subordination, and that,
if required by the lender, the Agency would agree to such
subordination.
At the February 28 neeting, the Agency considered the U.S.
Bancorp financing proposal for the construction financing of the
Project. The minutes indicate that the financing proposal
included a subordination condition by the lender and that this
item was thoroughly discussed by the Agency menbers.
At the close of its del,iberations, the Agency authorized by
a 4-0 vote the subrnission of the U.S. Bancorp loan application
with the subordination condition and the payrnent of $1OO,OO0 from
Agency funds for the loan processing fee. The minutes also
indicate that, while the Agency agreed to the subordination of
its fee interest to the construction Lender, it did reserve the
question of subordination with respect to the future permanent or
take-out financing which will occur after the project is built
out. There are no decisions pending before the Agency on the
question of future subordination.
U.S..Bancorp recentl,y approved the loan application for the
construction financing, and the pending decision on October 6,
1992 is for formal Agency approvil of the construction Financing
Plan which includes the U.S. Bancorp loan and of conforning
arnendnents to the DDA. The Agencyrs discretion in making i.his
decision is spetled out in the DDA in Section z.os. (pl. 'ti_tz ot
the DDA) and quoted in the City Attorney,s 1etter to'yiur ig".r"y.
Thank you for your tirne and attention to this natter.
Please feel free to contact me.if you would like uny-uJJiiion"r

Very truly yours,
I,EIDIGH
